dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hello, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I have chest pain. It started about 30 minutes ago.
The chest pain is sharp and in the center of my chest. It radiates to my right shoulder.
I also have lower back pain, but it's normal for me. I've been under a lot of stress.
I've also been urinating frequently lately.
Have you ever had heart trouble or other medical conditions?
I have mild asthma and hypothyroidism. I don't have any cardiac history.
Are you on any medications?
I take Singulair, Wellbutrin, Toradol, and Trexbrom.
Let me just check your responsiveness. Can you open your eyes and follow my voice?
Yes, I can see you.
And you're able to speak clearly. Tell me your full name and today's date.
Mary Johnson. October 20th, 2019. I've been having that back pain again.
You're alert and oriented with normal responses. No signs of confusion or delayed reactions.
Your airway is clear and open. I hear no signs of obstruction or unusual sounds.
Airway is patent, and she did not demonstrate any signs of difficulty maintaining it.
Let me listen to your breathing. Can you take a deep breath for me?
Respiratory rate is 20 breaths per minute, and depth is regular.
No cyanosis noted. Your lips and skin color appear normal.
Trachea is aligned centrally with no signs of deviation.
No signs of labored breathing or increased effort observed.
Lung sounds are clear in all lobes with no adventitious sounds.
Chest rise and fall are equal on both sides. No sign of localized restriction.
Oxygen saturation is at 99% using pulse oximetry.
Let me check your pulse and skin condition. How are you feeling right now?
Pulse rate is 82 beats per minute and regular.
Skin is pink, intact, and shows no signs of abnormal coloration.
Peripheral pulses are strong and regular in both arms and legs.
Your skin temperature feels normal and warm to the touch.
Blood pressure reading shows as 145 over 84.
Let's go through some more information to understand your condition better.
The pain you described earlier-has it changed at all since onset?
No. It started about 30 minutes ago and has remained constant.
Do you have any allergies?
No known drug or food allergies.
You mentioned medications earlier. Can you confirm which you take on a daily basis?
Singulair, Wellbutrin, Toradol, and Trexbrom.
Any heart issues in the past or other serious conditions?
No heart problems, but I do have hypothyroidism and mild asthma.
When did you last have something to eat or drink?
I had some tea around 5 PM.
What might have led up to the chest pain? Any unusual activities or changes?
I've been very stressed with work. I think that may be it.
When did the chest pain first begin?
About 30 minutes ago, when I was sitting at home.
Does the pain increase during movement or breathing?
It's more of a constant sharp pain. Not really worse during activity.
How would you describe the feeling?
It's sharp and stabbing, centered in the chest.
You mentioned it radiates to your right shoulder. Any other areas?
Just the right shoulder.
How severe is the pain on a 1 to 10 scale?
It's a 9.
Has the pain changed in intensity over the past 30 minutes?
No, it has stayed about the same.
Skin examination shows it to be warm, pink, and dry with no visible rashes, abrasions, or scars.
Pupils are equally reactive and round. No signs of neck masses or swelling observed.
Ears, nose, and throat are within normal limits. No drainage or obstructions noted.
Chest rising and falling symmetrically. Breath sounds remain clear and consistent bilaterally.
No deformities, angulation, or swelling seen in extremities. Movements are grossly intact.
Your pulse remains stable at 82 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 20 breaths per minute, unchanged from previous.
Blood pressure is recorded at 145 over 84.
Glucose level is 125, which is within the normal range.
Oxygen saturation is 99%, and remains stable.
We will be performing an EKG to assess heart rhythm.
Applying electrode pads across your chest for the ECG. This will help assess your heart activity.
Performing 4-lead ECG now. Rhythm is regular sinus.
Moving to 12-lead EKG for more detailed cardiac evaluation.
Initial results interpreted as borderline-possible right atrial enlargement, but no immediate cardiac issues detected.
We are setting up an IV in your right arm. It is 18-gauge, and can be used for fluids or medications.
Giving you 324 mg of aspirin orally as part of protocol for suspected cardiac chest pain.
Rechecking vitals: pulse now at 92, respiratory rate 19, and blood pressure 132 over 85.
Updated vitals: pulse 81, BP 135/76, and respiratory rate of 19. Oxygen saturation is 97%.
Final vitals are 77 for pulse, 129 over 77 for blood pressure, and respiratory rate of 20. Oxygen saturation is reported as 0%, but likely due to device malfunction or desaturation after transport.
Any updates on the chest pain's severity?
The pain intensity is at 9, just as before.
Any new developments regarding your medical condition or symptoms?
No changes, same symptoms and medication.
We will now assist you onto the stretcher. This is to ensure your safety and comfort during transport.
A 5-point seat belt will be used to keep you secure during the move and transport.
You are showing no signs of distress and are responding appropriately, so no specific interventions are urgent at this time.
Transportation destination is [COMPANY_NAME]. We will keep you in the stretcher the entire time.
All findings, vitals, and interventions have been captured. Patient was alert, oriented, and showed no life-threatening conditions.
All procedures including IV, EKG, and medications have been documented upon transfer to the ED team.
Final skin assessment was normal and consistent with previous findings.
